## 1. CLI Flags

### `next dev --inspect`

Enables the **Node.js V8 inspector** so you can attach a debugger (Chrome DevTools, VS Code breakpoints, etc.) to the running server process.

```bash
# package.json
"dev:inspect": "next dev --inspect"
```

- Opens a WebSocket on `ws://127.0.0.1:9229` by default
- Attach in Chrome via `chrome://inspect`
- Attach in VS Code via a `launch.json` entry with `"request": "attach"`
- This is for **server-side breakpoint debugging** â€” not bundle analysis, not logs

---

### `next build --debug`

Enables **verbose build output**. Prints the routing rules that Next.js applies silently by default:

- **Redirects** (including the auto-generated trailing-slash redirect)
- **Rewrites**
- **Headers**

```bash
# package.json
"debug": "next build --debug"
```

**Comparison â€” what you actually see:**

| Section | Normal build | `--debug` build |
|---|---|---|
| Route table | âś… | âś… |
| Redirects | âťŚ (hidden) | âś… shown |
| Rewrites | âťŚ (hidden) | âś… shown |
| Headers | âťŚ (hidden) | âś… shown |

**Real output from this project:**

```
Redirects
â”Ś source: /:path+/
â”ś destination: /:path+
â”” permanent: true
```

That trailing-slash redirect is auto-generated by Next.js â€” you'd never know it's there without `--debug`.

> Documented in: CLI reference (`next.mdx`) options table + `devIndicators.mdx` prose.  
> Not mentioned in the `/guides/debugging` article.

---

### `next build --debug-prerender`

Prints detailed prerender tracing â€” which pages were statically generated vs. dynamically rendered and why. Useful when a page is unexpectedly dynamic.

```bash
next build --debug-prerender
```

---

### `next build --debug-build-paths=<path>`

Shows which dynamic params were generated for a specific route during `generateStaticParams`.

```bash
next build --debug-build-paths=/blog/[slug]
```

---

### `npx next experimental-analyze`

Bundle analyzer â€” opens a visual treemap of your JS bundle. No config needed.

```bash
npx next experimental-analyze
```

---

## 2. `next.config.js` Options

### `logging.fetches`

Logs every `fetch()` call to the **terminal** during dev (and build). Shows URL, method, cache status.

```js
// next.config.js
logging: {
  fetches: {
    fullUrl: true,   // show full URL (not truncated)
  },
},
```

> This project already has this enabled.

---

### `browserDebugInfoInTerminal`

Forwards **browser console** output (`console.log`, errors, etc.) to the **terminal** as well.

```js
// next.config.js
browserDebugInfoInTerminal: true,
```

> **Not** currently in this project's config. Would require adding it.

---

## 3. Default Log Routing (no flags needed)

| Log type | Where it appears |
|---|---|
| Server component logs / errors | CLI terminal |
| Client component logs / errors | Browser console |
| Fetch logs (with `logging.fetches`) | CLI terminal |
| Browser console (with `browserDebugInfoInTerminal`) | CLI terminal too |

---

## 4. React Developer Tools

### React DevTools Browser Extension

Chrome/Firefox extension. Adds two panels to DevTools:

- **Components** â€” inspect component tree, props, state, hooks
- **Profiler** â€” record renders, see flame graph, rank by render time

Install: [React DevTools on Chrome Web Store](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/react-developer-tools/fmkadmapgofadopljbjfkapdkoienihi)

---

### React Performance Tracks (Chrome Performance panel)

**Dev-only** feature (disabled in production by default). Zero setup required in dev mode â€” just open Chrome DevTools â†’ Performance â†’ record a trace.

Adds React-specific lanes to the Chrome Performance panel:

| Track | What it shows |
|---|---|
| **Scheduler** | When React scheduled work |
| **Components** | When individual components rendered |
| **Server Components** | RSC fetch/render timing |
| **Server Requests** | Network requests initiated by RSCs |

> This is NOT a production monitoring tool. It's a dev-only profiling integration.  
> Docs: https://react.dev/reference/dev-tools/react-performance-tracks

---

## 5. This Project's Current Debug Setup

From `package.json`:

```json
"scripts": {
  "dev": "next dev",
  "dev:inspect": "next dev --inspect",
  "debug": "next build --debug",
  "build": "next build",
  "start": "next start",
  "lint": "next lint"
}
```

From `next.config.js` (relevant parts):

```js
logging: {
  fetches: { fullUrl: true }   // âś… fetch URLs logged to terminal
},
cacheComponents: true,
reactCompiler: true,
// browserDebugInfoInTerminal: not set (browser logs stay in browser)
```

---

## 6. Quick Decision Guide

> "What tool do I reach for?"

| Goal | Tool |
|---|---|
| Set a breakpoint in server code | `npm run dev:inspect` + attach debugger |
| See what redirects/rewrites are active | `npm run debug` (`next build --debug`) |
| See why a page is dynamic not static | `next build --debug-prerender` |
| Inspect component tree / props / state | React DevTools â†’ Components tab |
| Profile renders, find slow components | React DevTools â†’ Profiler tab |
| See React render timing in a flame graph | Chrome Performance panel (React tracks) |
| See what fetch calls are made server-side | `logging.fetches` in `next.config.js` |
| Forward browser logs to terminal | `browserDebugInfoInTerminal: true` |
| Analyze bundle size | `npx next experimental-analyze` |
